coredata.dc:title: Highly flexible polymer-carbon dot-ferric ion nanocomposite hydrogels displaying super stretchability, ultrahigh toughness, good self-recovery and shape memory performance
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yang
Li, Yaozong
Wang, Di
Zhou, Wuyi
Dong, Xianming
Zhou, Shiyao
Wang, Chaoyang
Yang, Zhuohong
coredata.dc:description: The super stretchability, ultrahigh toughness, good self-recovery and shape memory performance are very important for the practical application of hydrogels. However, most of hydrogels do not demonstrate the above-mentioned features at the same time. In this study, we develop a promising and versatile strategy for the synthesis of novel tough poly(acrylamide-co-acrylic acid)-carbon dot-ferric ion (PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+) nanocomposite (NC) hydrogel with a dual cross-linked network, which is triggered by carbon dots (C-dots) and Fe3+ ions as physical cross-linkers. The introduction of Fe3+ ions into the first C-dot cross-linked hydrogel can form strong coordinate bonds with COO− groups of PAmAc chains and C-dots, and obviously increase the mechanical properties of hydrogels. Varying C-dot content, Ac content, or immersing time in FeCl3 solution can facilely adjust the mechanical properties of hydrogels. At optimal conditions, the PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ NC hydrogel displays extremely balanced and high mechanical properties (the fracture strain of 2663%, fracture stress of 4.00MPa and toughness of 71.30MJ/m3), good self-recovery capacity (ca. 80.5% toughness recovery after 4h of resting) and fatigue resistance. Basing on the dynamic and reversible nature of coordination bonds, the PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ hydrogel also shows the stimulation-triggered shape memory performance. All the above results indicate that the introduction of strong coordinate bonds into NC hydrogel is a promising alternative method to fabricate dual cross-linked NC hydrogels with excellent comprehensive properties and functions, which have potential applications in load-bearing soft devices and soft actuators.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite hydrogels
Carbon dot
Ferric ion
Synergistic effects
High mechanical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Energy dissipation and self-recovery, and fatigue resistance of hydrogels

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Foundation of Guangdong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Programme of Guangdong Province Ministry of Transportation
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Foundation for Fundamental Research on Matter

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2015ZM158 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21474032 , 51673075 , 51703067 ), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2015M580720 ), the Science and Technology Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2016A050502044 ), Programme of Guangdong Province Ministry of Transportation ( 201502016 ) . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis scheme of CF-hydrogels with dual-crosslinked network using C-dots and Fe3+ ions as physical cross-linkers. (b) Digital photographs of C-hydrogel (left), Cf-hydrogel (middle) and CF-hydrogel (right). CF-hydrogels display highly flexible and tough properties to ensure different high-level deformations: (c–f) CF-hydrogels can be easily bended, knotted and twisted; (g) CF-hydrogel can withstand strong compressed force, and quickly recover its shape upon removing the compression force; (h) the knotted CF-hydrogel can also be stretched with a high strain.
The tensile properties of C-hydrogel, Cf-hydrogel, and CF-hydrogel: (a) stress-strain curves and (b) Young’s modulus and toughness values.

Tensile stress-strain curves (a), Young’s modulus and toughness (b) of CF-hydrogels with different C-dot contents. The Ac content and immersing time in FeCl3 solution were fixed at 15% and 8h, respectively.

Tensile stress-strain curves (a), Young’s modulus and toughness (b) of CF-hydrogels with different Ac contents. The C-dot content and immersing time in FeCl3 solution were fixed at 0.50wt% and 8h, respectively.
(a) Loading-unloading tensile stress-strain curves of C-hydrogel, Cf-hydrogel and CF-hydrogel under 1000% strain. (b) The total and dissipated toughness calculated from (a).
Self-recovery and loading-unloading tensile stress-strain curves of CF-hydrogel: (a) good self-recovery of the as-prepared samples; (b) the time-dependent recovery of hysteresis loop.
Ten successive loading-unloading stress-strain curves of the (a) as-prepared CF-hydrogel sample and (b) recovered CF-hydrogel sample (10h at room temperature).
The pictures of stimuli-triggered shape recovery process of hydrogel from the temporary helical shape to the original strip shape in 0.5mol/L HCl solution.

head.ce:title: Highly flexible polymer-carbon dot-ferric ion nanocomposite hydrogels displaying super stretchability, ultrahigh toughness, good self-recovery and shape memory performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Materials Science, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The super stretchability, ultrahigh toughness, good self-recovery and shape memory performance are very important for the practical application of hydrogels. However, most of hydrogels do not demonstrate the above-mentioned features at the same time. In this study, we develop a promising and versatile strategy for the synthesis of novel tough poly(acrylamide-co-acrylic acid)-carbon dot-ferric ion (PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+) nanocomposite (NC) hydrogel with a dual cross-linked network, which is triggered by carbon dots (C-dots) and Fe3+ ions as physical cross-linkers. The introduction of Fe3+ ions into the first C-dot cross-linked hydrogel can form strong coordinate bonds with COO− groups of PAmAc chains and C-dots, and obviously increase the mechanical properties of hydrogels. Varying C-dot content, Ac content, or immersing time in FeCl3 solution can facilely adjust the mechanical properties of hydrogels. At optimal conditions, the PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ NC hydrogel displays extremely balanced and high mechanical properties (the fracture strain of 2663%, fracture stress of 4.00MPa and toughness of 71.30MJ/m3), good self-recovery capacity (ca. 80.5% toughness recovery after 4h of resting) and fatigue resistance. Basing on the dynamic and reversible nature of coordination bonds, the PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ hydrogel also shows the stimulation-triggered shape memory performance. All the above results indicate that the introduction of strong coordinate bonds into NC hydrogel is a promising alternative method to fabricate dual cross-linked NC hydrogels with excellent comprehensive properties and functions, which have potential applications in load-bearing soft devices and soft actuators.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer hydrogels consisting of abundant water in the three-dimensional polymer networks [1,2] have attracted increasing interest in the biomedical, agricultural and industrial applications including separation [3,4], drug delivery [5–7], sensing [8,9], actuation [10,11] and tissue engineering [12–14]. However, most of synthetic hydrogels usually demonstrate poor mechanical properties [15] due to the negligible energy dissipation in their inhomogeneity network structures under deformation, which greatly limits their practical applications for high load-bearing aspects [16]. Hence, significant efforts have recently been thrown into design and develop unique network structures and energy dissipation modes [17] for preparing the tough hydrogels with high mechanical properties, such as nanocomposite (NC) hydrogels [18–20], ionically cross-linked hydrogels [21], double-network hydrogels [22–24] and dual cross-linked hydrogels [25,26]. Among various tough hydrogels, NC hydrogels with unique structures and high stretchability are one type of the most widely explored tough hydrogels. In NC hydrogels, the strong hybrid polymer networks can be formed via physically cross-linking the flexible polymer chains using the uniformly dispersed nanoparticles as physical cross-linkers [27–29] and nanofillers, which can effectively combine the beneficial properties of the nanoparticles and polymers to achieve a clear synergistic effect. In this aspect, the nanoparticles can serve as stress transfer centers to efficiently relax the external stress for the polymer matrix, and thus delay complete fractures of the polymer chains [30].

Carbon nanodots (C-dots) asa new type of carbon nanomaterials have obtained increasing attention for the applications including bioimaging, sensing and catalysis fields, due to their exceptional properties such as highly fluorescent feature, low toxicity, excellent dispersibility in water, flexible functionalization and good biocompatibility [31–35]. Moreover, similar to cellulose nanocrystals, clay and graphene oxide (GO), C-dots possess numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces such as hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and epoxide groups [36], which indicates that C-dots have the promising potential application as physical cross-linkers in the preparation of NC hydrogels [37]. Thus, C-dots are also used to act as physical cross-linkers for fabricating polyacrylamide (PAM)/C-dot NC hydrogels in our research group [38,39]. The fracture strain (elongation) of prepared PAM/C-dot hydrogels could achieve more than 4000%, but they were relatively soft and their tensile stresses were smaller than 0.2MPa. Thus, it is very essential to develop effective approach to fabricate the C-dot cross-linked NC hydrogels with extraordinary comprehensive mechanical property.

Recently, the non-covalent coordinate bonds (formed by Fe3+ ions) with relatively high binding strength have been introduced into the polymer network of NC hydrogels to obtain single network with dual cross-links [40], which can substantially enhance the mechanical properties of NC hydrogels by the synergistic effects of NC and dual-crosslinked hydrogels. Zhao et al. have developed a tough poly(acrylic acid) (PAA)-GO NC hydrogel by introducing coordinate bonds interacted between Fe3+ ions and the carboxylic groups in the hydrogel polymer chains. And the tensile strength of the tough PAA/GO NC hydrogel can attain 2.5MPa with the fracture strain of 700% [41]. Furthermore, our previous study has displayed the tough poly(acrylamide-co-acrylic acid) (PAmAc)-clay NC hydrogel prepared by introducing Fe3+ ions to further interact with the PAmAc polymer chains including carboxylic groups. The as-prepared tough hydrogel can attain the ultrahigh fracture stress of ca. 3.5MPa and fracture strain of ca. 2100%, respectively [42]. To this end, it is very promising possibility for improving the C-dot cross-linked NC hydrogels containing carboxylic groups by further crosslinking with Fe3+ ions to form the strong coordinate bonds.

In this study, we prepared novel poly(acrylamide-co-acrylic acid)-carbon dot-ferric ion (PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+) NC hydrogels with dual cross-linking effects by the introduction of Fe3+ ions into the PAmAc-Cdot NC hydrogel polymer chains to form coordinate bonds. To be specific, we firstly prepared the PAmAc-Cdot NC hydrogels by the in situ copolymerization of acrylamide (Am) and acrylic acid (Ac) in the C-dot aqueous suspension. Afterwards, the coordinate bonds were introduced by soaking the PAmAc-Cdot hydrogels into FeCl3 solution, and then reorganized to form more tridentate coordinate bonds. The effects of crucial factors such asC-dot content, Ac content, or immersing time in FeCl3 solution on the mechanical properties of the PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ NC hydrogels were systematically discussed, and the self-recovery capacity and fatigue resistance of the optimal PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ NC hydrogels were further studied. Furthermore, considering that the coordination bonds formed between Fe3+ ions and carboxylic groups be adjusted because of the reversible ion exchange of Fe3+ and H+ ions, the stimulation-triggered shape memory ability of PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ NC hydrogels was also investigated.

β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) was obtained from Shanghai Boao Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was supplied with Guangzhou Chemical Factory (Guangzhou, China). C-dots were prepared using β-CDas carbon source via low temperature hydrothermal method basing on our previous report [39], and the mean particle size of spherical C-dots was approximately 3.5nm. Acrylamide (Am) was provided by Shanghai Rich Joint Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acrylic acid (Ac), ammonium persulfate (APS) and iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were bought from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Factory (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were applied as received without any purification.

The PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ hydrogels were synthesized using physical crosslinkers without any covalent crosslinker, and the detailed preparation procedures were presented as follows. Firstly, certain amount of C-dots was added in water and uniformly dispersed under ultrasonication for 10min to obtain C-dot suspension (0.25, 0.50, 1.00 or 2.00wt%, weight ratio of C-dots/water). Afterwards, the monomers Am (3mol/L, molar volume of C-dots in water) and Ac (10, 15, 20, or 25%, with respect to the molar ratio of Am), initiator APS (0.1wt%, weight ratio of APS/water) were added respectively into the C-dot suspension, and then the suspension system was sonicated in an ice-water bath for 30min. After the above suspension system was deaired for 5min under N2 atmosphere, the resulted suspension was injected into cylinder glass molds (3.2mm in the diameter) with sealed plastic wraps, and then placed into a water bath at 45°C for 24h to form the PAmAc-Cdot hydrogels (named asC-hydrogels, which meant only crosslinking by C-dots). Furthermore, the C- hydrogels were soaked into FeCl3 solution (0.06mol/L) for several hours (2, 4, 8 or 16h) to introduce Fe3+ ions interacted with COO− groups and obtain the original PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ hydrogels (coded as Cf-hydrogels, which meant dual-crosslinking by C-dots and Fe3+ ions at the transition state). Finally, the Cf-hydrogels were immersed for 24h in water for getting rid of superfluous Fe3+ ions to produce the reorganized PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ hydrogels (denoted as CF-hydrogels, which referred to dual-crosslinking by C-dots and Fe3+ ions at the reorganization state). Unless specifically noted, the C-dot content, Ac content and immersing time in FeCl3 solution were 0.5wt%, 15% and 8h, respectively.

The mechanical properties of hydrogels were measured with a Shimadzu universal material testing machine equipped with an AG-X plus testing system at ambient temperature. For tensile tests, the initial sample length between the two jaws was about 20mm, and the crosshead speed was 100mm/min. For avoiding water evaporation, before successive loading-unloading tests, the surfaces of the hydrogel samples were coated with silicone oil, and sealed with plastic wraps. The sample engineering strain was regarded as the length change related to the original length, and the engineering stress was evaluated basing on the force divided by the cross-sectional area of the original sample. Unless otherwise stated, in this study the engineering strain and engineering stress were denoted as strain and stress, respectively. The Young’s modulus was assessed using the slope of initial linear region in the stress-strain profile, and the toughness (or dissipated energy) was defined as the area below the stress-strain profiles for uniaxial tests (or between the loading-unloading profiles for loading-unloading tests). The recovery ratio of hydrogel sample was calculated as the dissipated energy ratio of the recovery sample/original sample. At least three samples were measured for each hydrogel, and the mean values of Young’s modulus, toughness and recovery ratio were calculated.

The shape memory behavior of hydrogel was studied as follows. The straight strip of C-hydrogel was firstly deformed into a temporary shape by external force, and then fixed by soaking in FeCl3 aqueous solution (0.06mol/L) and pure water for a certain time. And the shape recovery phenomenon was performed by immersing the hydrogel sample with temporary shape in HCl solution (0.5mol/L).

Fig. 1
a shows the synthesis method for the preparation of the tough CF-hydrogels. In the first step, C-dot crosslinked NC hydrogels (C-hydrogels) were synthesized by in situ free radical polymerization of acrylamide (Am) and acrylic acid (Ac) in C-dot suspension. Herein, C-dots were served as physical cross-linkers, which was due to the reason that the oxygen-containing groups (including hydroxyl and carboxy groups) on the C-dot surfaces could form hydrogen bonds with the amide and carboxy groups of the PAmAc polymer chains. The prepared C-hydrogels displayed transparent uniformity when being viewed with the naked eyes (see Fig. 1b). In the second step, C-hydrogels were immersed into FeCl3 aqueous solution to form Cf-hydrogels by strong coordination bonds between Fe3+ and COO − groups from PAmAc polymer side chains and C-dot surfaces, and then soaked in water to obtain the CF-hydrogels. For the presence of Fe3+ ions, the Cf-hydrogels and CF-hydrogels displayed a red color (see Fig. 1b), which was different from transparent C-hydrogels, showing successful incorporation of Fe3+ ions into C-hydrogels. The soaking process in water was performed because superfluous Fe3+ ions in the hydrogels produced a mixture of mono-, bi-, or tridentates [43] with COO − groups from PAmAc polymer side chains and C-dot surfaces. For producing more tridentates to obtain the further improved mechanical properties of hydrogels, the superfluous Fe3+ ions were removed by the soaking process in water, and the ion coordinates were reorganized to form more tridentates.

The CF-hydrogels were highly flexible and mechanical properties, and they were robust enough to endure high deformations. As shown in Fig. 1c–f, the CF-hydrogels could be flexibly bended, knotted and twisted into diverse shapes, which indicated their outstanding elasticity (similar to elastomers) and toughness, high processability. Moreover, it can be observed from Fig. 1g that the CF-hydrogel could be compressed to an extremely high strain without any observed damage, and quickly recover to its original cylinder shape upon the removal of compression stress, demonstrating its excellently toughness and good self-recoverability. What was interesting, the knotted CF-hydrogel could be stretched to about 7 times of its initial length without any necking phenomena and damages (see Fig. 1h), fully showing its high stretchability.

The quantitative investigations of mechanical properties for the hydrogels were performed via a Shimadzu universal material testing machine. Fig. 2 shows the typical tensile stress-strain curves, Young’s modulus and toughness of C-hydrogel, Cf-hydrogel and CF-hydrogel. The C-hydrogel was rather soft and ductile with the large strain at break of ca. 3900%, low stress at break of 0.056MPa, Young’s modulus of 0.013MPa and toughness of 1.21MJ/m3, which should be ascribed that there was only C-dot cross-linking to form the physically crosslinked PAmAc network. By being immersed in the FeCl3 solution, the Cf-hydrogel could be greatly reinforced with the strain at break of 3028%, stress at break of 2.27MPa, Young’s modulus of 0.28MPa and toughness of 43.44MJ/m3, which should be because that the carboxyl groups at PAmAc chains and C-dot surfaces could be physically cross-linked with Fe3+ by strong ionic coordinate interaction to enhance the tensile strength and toughness. Moreover, after the immersing process in water, the CF-hydrogel was slightly faded and exhibited stronger mechanical properties than Cf-hydrogel. To be specific, the stress at break, Young’s modulus, and toughness of CF-hydrogel were ca. 1.8, 2.9 and 1.6 times as those of Cf-hydrogel, respectively, which should be due to the fact that more tridentates formed by diffusing excess Fe3+ out of hydrogel resulted in the further enhancement of mechanical properties. It was worth noting that the stress at break, Young’s modulus, and toughness of CF-hydrogel were ca. 71.4, 62.4 and 58.9 times as those of C-hydrogel, respectively, indicating that the introduction and regeneration of coordinate bonds greatly enhanced the mechanical properties of the C-hydrogel. Furthermore, compared to the PAmAc-clay NC hydrogel crosslinking with clays and Fe3+ ions in our previous work [42], the CF-hydrogel showed higher stretchability and toughness. This result might be ascribed that compared to the sheet clays, the approximately spherical C-dots were easy movement along the PAmAc chains and served as lubricants in stretching process [39], leading to the larger movement strain and toughness. In addition, due to the presence of strong coordinate bonds, the volume change of CF-hydrogel was greatly limited after soaking in water at room temperature for 2months, and evenly the mechanical properties of CF-hydrogel just decreased slightly (Fig. S1). The above results indicated that the structure of CF-hydrogels was very stable.

The mechanical properties of hydrogels are very vital to influence the practical application fields. Herein, the influence of the C-dot content, Ac content and immersing time in FeCl3 solution on the mechanical properties of the hydrogels was gradually investigated. Fig. 3 displays the tensile properties of CF-hydrogels prepared with different C-dot contents. It was observed that when the C-dot content increased from 0.25wt% to 2wt%, the fracture strain greatly and monotonically decreased from 2760% to 1596%, while the fracture stress and Young’s modulus gradually increased from 3.63 to 4.23MPa and from 0.55 to 1.35MPa, respectively. And increasing C-dot content from 0.25 to 0.5wt% led to the enhancement of hydrogel toughness from 61.75 to 71.30MJ/m3, but further increasing C-dot content to 2.0wt%, the toughness obviously decreased to 49.18MJ/m3. As mentioned above, C-dots were served as physical cross-linkers for the production of C-hydrogels. With the increase in C-dot content, more C-dots could be used to physically crosslink and form hydrogen bonding interaction with PAmAc polymer chains, which resulted in the increase of the crosslinking density and the production of more robust network structure, and in turn led to the shortening of PAmAc polymer chains between crosslinking points. Thus increasing C-dot content caused the increase in the stretchability and decrease in the fracture stress and Young’s modulus of hydrogels. Overall, the tensile properties of CF-hydrogels were obviously influenced by C-dot content, and a moderate C-dot content (0.5wt%) could be applied to synthesize CF-hydrogel with significant tensile properties.

The influence of the Ac contents on the tensile properties of CF-hydrogels was investigated as shown in Fig. 4 . As observed, with the increase of Ac content from 10% to 15%, the toughness increased from 55.88 to 71.30MJ/m3. With further increase of Ac content from 15% to 25%, the toughness clearly lowered from 71.30 to 41.08MJ/m3. In addition, with increasing Ac contents from 10% from 25%, the fracture stress and Young’s modulus increased from 3.02 to 4.41MPa and from 0.39 to 1.88MPa, respectively, but the fracture strain distinctly decreased from 2815% to 1243%. The above results might be ascribed that with the increase of Ac content, more carboxyl groups from Ac (see Fig. S2) could interact with C-dots and Fe3+ to form more hydrogen bonds and coordination bonds in polymer chains, which resulted in the increase of crosslinking density of hydrogels. With low crosslinking density, the CF-hydrogels showed the relatively low tensile strength and relatively high elongation. On the contrary, with high crosslinking density, the CF-hydrogels became stiff and easily fracture at low strain. As discussed above, a moderate Ac content (15%) could be applied to obtain a suitable crosslinking density, and thus form highly tough and super stretchable CF-hydrogel.

We further investigated the influence of immersing time in FeCl3 solution on the tensile properties of CF-hydrogels (Fig. S3). It was seen that increasing immersing time in FeCl3 solution (from 2 to 16h) resulted in the clear increase of fracture stress (from 2.72 to 4.43MPa) and Young’s modulus (from 0.27 to 0.89MPa), but the distinct decrease of fracture strain (from 3064% to 1798%). And the toughness of hydrogels increased (from 50.24 to 71.30MJ/m3) with the increase of immersing time in FeCl3 solution (from 2 to 8h), while decreased (to 55.56MJ/m3) when immersing time in FeCl3 solution further enhanced (to 16h). With prolonging immersing time in FeCl3 solution, more ionic coordinates could be formed between Fe3+ and COO − groups from PAmAc polymer side chains and C-dot surfaces, leading to the increase of the crosslinking degree. It was found that too high or too low crosslinking degree resulted in unbalanced tensile properties of CF-hydrogels, thus an optimal immersing time in FeCl3 solution (8h) could achieve the balanced high tensile properties.

Basing on the results mentioned above, the tensile properties of CF-hydrogels could be easily adjusted by varying c-dot content, Ac content, or immersing time in FeCl3 solution. And the optimal parameters (C-dot content of 0.5wt%, Ac content of 15% and immersing time in FeCl3 solution of 8h) were applied to obtain the optimal CF-hydrogels with the balanced high tensile properties (the fracture strain of 2663%, fracture stress of 4.00MPa, Young’s modulus of 0.81MPa and toughness of 71.30MJ/m3). Moreover, the tensile properties of Cf-hydrogels prepared with different C-dot content, Ac content or immersing time in FeCl3 solution were also investigated (Fig. S4–S6). It was found that the tensile properties (fracture stress, Young’s modulus, and toughness) of CF-hydrogel were clearly higher than those of the related Cf-hydrogel, which indicated that in this study the water treatment of Cf-hydrogels had a vital influence on their tensile performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy dissipation and self-recovery, and fatigue resistance of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 compared the loading-unloading stress-strain profiles under 1000% strain of the C-hydrogel, Cf-hydrogel, CF-hydrogel, which could display their energy dissipation capacity and in turn further evaluate their respective toughness. It was seen that the loading profile and unloading profile of C-hydrogel almost overlapped. Whereas, the partial enlarged graph of the loading-unloading stress-strain profiles of the C-hydrogel (Fig. S7) displayed a diminutive hysteresis loop, indicating low energy dissipation upon deformation. Compared with C-hydrogel, the Cf-hydrogel and CF-hydrogel clearly possessed large hysteresis loops. Consistently, the energy dissipations of Cf-hydrogel (6.54MJ/m3) and CF-hydrogel (14.7MJ/m3) were much bigger than that of C-hydrogel (0.07MJ/m3). The above results showed the larger and more effective energy dissipation (high toughness) of Cf-hydrogel and CF-hydrogel upon deformation. Thus, the strong coordinate bonds between Fe3+ ions and COO− groups would offer additional capacity for greatly enhancing toughness of hydrogels. In addition, the CF-hydrogel showed the larger hysteresis loop and dissipated the higher energy than Cf-hydrogel, indicating CF-hydrogel possessed higher toughness, which clearly improved due to the vital role of tridentate coordinate bond interaction.

The network of CF-hydrogel was physically dual-linked via reversible noncovalent interactions, and the ruptured noncovalent bonds could be recovered at suitable conditions. Herein, the loading-unloading tests under 800% were performed to examine the self-recovery capacity of CF-hydrogel at different resting times without any external stimuli. It was observed from Fig. 6
a that when the two consecutive loading-unloading tests were conducted without any resting time, the hysteresis loop became clearly small, and the dissipated energy was greatly lost from the first loading-unloading test (10.9MJ/m3) to the immediate second loading- unloading test (3.2MJ/m3). And with the prolongation of the resting time, the related hysteresis loops became larger and dissipated energy also gradually enhanced, indicating the gradual recovery of the CF-hydrogel with the increase in the resting time. The second loading-unloading test was performed after resting 10min, the toughness recovery ratio was 45.7%, while the second loading-unloading test was conducted after resting 4h, the toughness recovery ratio was up to 80.5%. The above results indicated that the CF-hydrogel displayed the good self-recovery, which was much related with the good fatigue resistance of hydrogels.

Furthermore, the CF-hydrogel suffered from enough fatigue via ten successive loading-unloading tests under the strain of 800% to verify its favorable fatigue resistance. It was seen from Fig. 7 that after the first ten successive loading-unloading tests, the CF-hydrogel sample was without any obvious damage, and the tensile stress and toughness could recover effectively and even increased slightly after resting 10h at room temperature, clearly showing the fatigue resistance of CF-hydrogel.

Basing on these above results, the dual-crosslinked NC hydrogels possessed not only highly mechanical strength, stretchability, and toughness, but also good self-recovery property and fatigue resistance. The ultrahigh mechanical properties of CF-hydrogels were largely attributed to their unique network structures and effective energy dissipation mechanism. To be specific, obvious synergistic effects existed in the CF-hydrogels among the C-dots, PAmAc polymer chains and Fe3+ ions: (i) C-dots as physical cross-linkers crosslinked with PAmAc polymer chains by hydrogen bonds, and also as stress transfer centers for excellent mechanical strength; (ii) PAmAc polymer chains as the polymer matrix afforded favorable ductility; (iii) Fe3+ ions as physical cross-linkers offered dynamic and reversible cross-linking by coordinate bonds with COO− groups of PAmAc chains and C-dot surfaces, resulting in the dual crosslinked effects. Upon the loading of external force, the strong coordinate bonds could dynamically fracture to effectively dissipate energy, and C-dots coordinated with PAmAc chains safeguarded the hydrogel network configuration and served as stress transfer centers to efficiently transfer stress for the polymer matrix. In addition, upon the removal of external force, the fractured coordinate bonds could rapidly recombine without any external stimuli to recover and homogenize the hydrogel networks. In this point, the dynamic and recoverable dual cross-linked synergistic effects could endow highly mechanical strength, super toughness and stretchability, good self-recovery and fatigue resistance of CF-hydrogels.

The hydrogel networks contain carboxylate groups, which could chelate Fe3+ to form coordinate bonds and greatly enhance the mechanical properties of hydrogels. Moreover, the coordinate bonds between Fe3+ and COO − groups were reversible basing on the reversible ion exchange of Fe3+ and H+ ions within the hydrogels [44]. The reversible coordinate bonds could also be employed to maintain the temporary shape of hydrogels and attain the stimulation-triggered shape memory property [45]. Herein, the straight strip of C-hydrogel was shaped into a helical shape, and then soaked into FeCl3 solution (0.06mol/L) for 8h and pure water for 24h. Through the coordinations of COO− groups and Fe3+, the mechanical property of the hydrogel obviously enhanced, and the temporary helical shape could be well fixed. Subsequently, by soaked in 0.5mol/L HCl, the tough CF-hydrogel could become softened and faded color from brown to light yellow, at the same time the temporary helical shape was gradually relaxed and correspondingly recovered to the permanent shape (Fig. 8 ) within 62min. The above mentioned shape fixation and relaxation were reversible by immersing the hydrogel in FeCl3 aqueous solution and HCl solution, respectively, which could be repeated for more than three times.

In conclusion, we had synthesized a kind of tough PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+ NC hydrogels with dual-crosslinked network by first cross-linking of hydrogen bonds formed between C-dots and PAmAc polymer chains, and second cross-linking of strong coordinate bonds formed between Fe3+ ions and COO− groups of PAmAc polymer chains and C-dot surfaces. The introduction of reversible cross-linking of strong coordinate bonds could effectively dissipate energy and recover the hydrogel networks, thus resulting in the obvious increase in the mechanical properties of hydrogels. The mechanical performances of CF-hydrogels were easily tunable in a wide range by changing C-dot content, Ac content, or immersing time in FeCl3 solution. At optimal conditions, the CF-hydrogels could achieve extremely balanced and high mechanical properties (the fracture strain of 2663%, fracture stress of 4.00MPa and toughness of 71.30MJ/m3). Furthermore, because of the unique network structures and reversible strong coordinate bonds, CF-hydrogels also showed fast sufficient self-recovery and excellent fatigue resistance properties without any external stimuli, and stimulation-triggered shape memory performance. Overall, all these results indicated that this tough dual-crosslinked NC hydrogels with prominent mechanical properties and function should be promising materials for applications such as in load-bearing soft devices and soft actuators.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2015ZM158), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21474032, 51673075, 51703067), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2015M580720), the Science and Technology Foundation of Guangdong Province (2016A050502044), Programme of Guangdong Province Ministry of Transportation (201502016) .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2017.08.044.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary data 1
.
